risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups." Comment: The paper states that allocation was random, but provides no details on how the random sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, blocks, lots, etc.). 'Randomly allocated' alone without additional context is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, and there is no explicit evidence of a non-random method.

Quote: "The subjects were randomly allocated to either of the two treatment groups." Comment: The report states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled assignment, or another method preventing the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Group A (conventional treatment group): Subjects in this group received deep heating modality... and free exercises..." and "Group B (conventional treatment and retro-walking group): Subjects underwent three sessions of Retro-walking per day... along with conventional treatment..." Also, "The two patient reported outcomes were taken before treatment, after 1 week of treatment and after 3 weeks of treatment" and outcomes included VAS and WOMAC, both patient-reported. Comment: The interventions were visibly different (retro-walking added in one group), so participants and treating personnel would be aware of group assignment; no blinding procedure is described. Because the main outcomes were subjective patient-reported pain and function/disability measures, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "The tools used for measurement of the two primary outcomes: (1) A 10 cm visual analogue scale (VAS) for rating the intensity of perceived pain... The patient was asked to mark his/her pain... (2) Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Arthritis Index (WOMAC) of OA, a patient reported scale, was used to assess pain, stiffness and physical function levels in the subjects." Quote: "The two patient reported outcomes were taken before treatment, after 1 week of treatment and after 3 weeks of treatment." Quote: "Group B (conventional treatment and retro-walking group) Subjects underwent three sessions of Retro-walking per day... along with conventional treatment..." Comment: The outcome assessor for the most vulnerable outcomes was the patient (self-report). VAS and WOMAC are subjective patient-reported outcomes, and participants would necessarily know whether they were receiving retro-walking in addition to conventional treatment versus conventional treatment alone. Under the worst-outcome rule, these unblinded subjective outcomes make detection bias high risk overall, even though ROM/strength were also measured.

Quote: "Thirty three patients fulfilling the inclusion criteria were screened and included in study after obtaining their consent. Three patients were lost to follow-up. The study population thus had 30 adults... Scores were analyzed pre intervention, at the end of 1 week and at the end of 3 weeks in both the groups." Comment: The paper reports 3 post-randomization losses overall (33 to 30 analyzed, about 9%), but does not report how these losses were distributed by randomized group, at which follow-up time point(s) they occurred, or the reasons for loss. Because per-arm analysed numbers on the outcomes of interest are not provided, attrition cannot be adequately judged as balanced/unbalanced; therefore this domain is unclear rather than high.
